description: Trace Module 2 — Synthesizer. Converts a recorded trajectory (produced by Module 1 / Recorder) into a human-readable SKILL.md + machine-readable skill.meta.json, via a two-phase pipeline: (phase 1) a single multimodal Claude Sonnet 4.5 call consumes events.jsonl + sampled keyframe screenshots and produces a draft skill + up to 5 targeted follow-up questions; (phase 2) the UI walks the user through the questions one at a time via server-sent events, feeding each answer back to a revision call that returns an updated draft; (phase 3) the user approves and the final skill is written to disk. Runs as a Python 3.11 FastAPI service mounted at /synthesize, /skills, and /trajectories-consumer on the gateway on 127.0.0.1:8765. Reads trajectories from ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/trajectories/<uuid>/ (never mutates them). Writes skills to ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/skills/<slug>/. This module owns the SKILL.md format specification (downstream Runner parses it strictly). Destructive-step flagging happens in two places: the LLM is instructed to flag via ⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE] in markdown AND a keyword matcher runs over every step target label as a secondary check — mismatches are surfaced as errors rather than silently resolved. Does NOT drive any real UI actions — pure distillation. Runs entirely on Claude Sonnet 4.5 (claude-sonnet-4-5); no OpenAI or other provider dependencies.

techStack.streamingProtocol: Server-Sent Events (SSE) for Q&A session updates to the UI
techStack.testing: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx (for mocking anthropic HTTP calls)
techStack.storage: filesystem under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/skills/ + a SQLite index shared with the recorder's index.db
techStack.fixtures: shared fixtures/ directory with the recorder; reads from fixtures/trajectories/*, writes golden outputs to fixtures/skills/*

outputStructure.SKILL.md: Human-readable markdown with strict structural format (sections: title H1, one-line description, ## Parameters, ## Preconditions, ## Steps (numbered 1-indexed), ## Expected outcome, ## Notes). Runner parses this.
outputStructure.skill.meta.json: Machine-readable metadata: slug, name, trajectory_id, created_at, parameters (name/type/required/default), destructive_steps (list of int step numbers), preconditions (list of strings), step_count. Schema at contracts/skill-meta.schema.json.
outputStructure.preview/: Optional directory with 3–5 representative screenshots copied from the trajectory for UI display. Screenshots are not part of the Runner contract.

skillMdInvariants: Steps are numbered sequentially starting at 1 and are never skipped or duplicated
Destructive steps are prefixed with '⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE]' in the markdown AND listed by number in skill.meta.json's destructive_steps array — both must agree
Parameter references in step text use {param_name} syntax with only [a-z0-9_] in names
Every parameter referenced in SKILL.md appears in skill.meta.json parameters array, and vice versa
Slugs match regex ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{2,39}$ and are unique within the skills directory

userStories.title: Package scaffold under the uv monorepo workspace
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the services/synthesizer Python package scaffolded under the shared uv workspace, with its own pyproject.toml entries, mypy strict config, ruff config, and pytest config, so the Ralph loop can run quality checks from iteration one.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/ exists with src layout (src/synthesizer/__init__.py, src/synthesizer/__main__.py, src/synthesizer/api.py placeholder)
Root pyproject.toml's [tool.uv.workspace] members list includes services/synthesizer
services/synthesizer/pyproject.toml declares dependencies: fastapi, uvicorn, anthropic>=0.40, jsonschema, pydantic>=2, sse-starlette
Dev dependencies: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx, mypy, ruff
mypy configured for --strict on synthesizer package; anthropic.* has its own type overrides only where needed
ruff config inherits from root (line length 100, target-version py311)
pytest discovers tests in tests/synthesizer/; tests/synthesizer/__init__.py and tests/synthesizer/conftest.py exist
tests/synthesizer/conftest.py exposes an anthropic_mock fixture using respx that intercepts api.anthropic.com calls
uv sync completes without error and installs the synthesizer package in editable mode
uv run ruff check services/synthesizer passes
uv run mypy --strict services/synthesizer passes
uv run pytest tests/synthesizer/ exits 0 ('no tests collected' is acceptable)
services/synthesizer/README.md exists with a one-paragraph description and a link to CLAUDE.md at repo root
userStories.notes: Foundation story. No functional code. The respx fixture is critical — every subsequent story that tests an LLM call will use it, so getting it right here saves rework. Do NOT add ANTHROPIC_API_KEY anywhere — that's read from env at runtime only.

userStories.title: skill.meta.json JSON schema
userStories.description: As downstream consumers (Runner, UI, fixture tests), I need a locked JSON schema for skill.meta.json so the meta file shape is enforceable and stable across modules.
acceptanceCriteria: contracts/skill-meta.schema.json exists at repo root as a valid JSON Schema draft 2020-12

Schema requires: slug (string, pattern ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{2,39}$), name (string, 1-100 chars), trajectory_id (uuid string), created_at (date-time string), parameters (array), destructive_steps (array of integers), preconditions (array of strings), step_count (integer, minimum 1)
parameters items require: name (string, pattern ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{0,29}$), type (enum: string/integer/boolean), required (boolean); optional default (string|integer|boolean|null)
additionalProperties: false on the top-level object to catch accidental key typos
services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/schema.py exposes load_meta_schema() and validate_meta(meta_dict) using jsonschema; raises ValidationError with a structured message on failure

services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/schema.py also exposes validate_meta_against_markdown(meta_dict, markdown_str) that cross-checks invariants: every parameter referenced in markdown is in meta.parameters, every destructive_step in meta matches a ⚠️ line in markdown, step_count matches number of numbered steps
tests/synthesizer/test_schema.py: 5+ valid example meta dicts pass validation

tests/synthesizer/test_schema.py: 10+ malformed meta dicts fail with specific, useful error messages (bad slug pattern, missing required, wrong type, extra key, destructive_steps out of range, parameter name with hyphens, etc.)
tests/synthesizer/test_schema.py: cross-check validator catches markdown/meta mismatches (markdown has ⚠️ on step 3 but meta.destructive_steps = [], and vice versa)
tests/synthesizer/test_schema.py: cross-check validator catches parameter mismatches (markdown references {foo} but meta.parameters doesn't list foo)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is a locked contract once Runner has shipped. The cross-check validator is load-bearing: the LLM will produce drift between markdown and meta, and we catch it structurally rather than hoping for prompt obedience. Do NOT relax the additionalProperties: false — it catches bugs early.

userStories.title: SKILL.md parser and renderer (round-trip)
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a parser that reads a SKILL.md into a structured ParsedSkill object and a renderer that writes a ParsedSkill back to SKILL.md, so I can programmatically manipulate skills (revise drafts, inject confirmed parameters) without regex-hacking markdown in multiple places.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/skill_doc.py exposes ParsedSkill dataclass (pydantic model) with fields: title (str), description (str), parameters (list of Parameter), preconditions (list of str), steps (list of Step), expected_outcome (str), notes (str|None)
Step dataclass has: number (int), text (str), destructive (bool)
Parameter dataclass has: name (str), type (str), required (bool), default (str|int|bool|None), description (str|None)
parse_skill_md(markdown: str) -> ParsedSkill handles the strict section order (title, description, ## Parameters, ## Preconditions, ## Steps, ## Expected outcome, ## Notes)

parse_skill_md raises SkillParseError with {section: str, reason: str, line: int} on malformed input (missing section, steps not 1-indexed, out-of-order numbers, non-integer destructive step reference in text)
render_skill_md(parsed: ParsedSkill) -> str produces canonical markdown: one blank line between sections, steps numbered 1..N, destructive steps prefixed with ⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE]
Round-trip property: for any ParsedSkill p, parse_skill_md(render_skill_md(p)) == p (implement as a test with 5+ example skills)

Parameter reference extraction: extract_parameter_refs(markdown) -> set[str] returns all {param_name} references found in step text (must handle multiple refs per step, ignore braces inside code blocks, ignore escaped \{)
tests/synthesizer/test_skill_doc.py: round-trip test on the 5 reference workflow golden skills (see S-016)
tests/synthesizer/test_skill_doc.py: malformed input tests for each documented SkillParseError case (at least 8 failure modes)
tests/synthesizer/test_skill_doc.py: extract_parameter_refs tests including edge cases (code blocks, escaped braces, multiple refs per step, nested braces not valid)
tests/synthesizer/test_skill_doc.py: render output is byte-for-byte stable (no trailing whitespace drift, consistent blank lines)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The Runner depends on this parser to extract steps in order. The round-trip test is the strongest single test in this module — it catches subtle drift that would otherwise show up as mystery bugs in the Runner three weeks later. Do NOT use a generic markdown library (commonmark, mistune) that normalizes whitespace aggressively — roll a small, predictable hand-parser that preserves structure.

userStories.title: Trajectory reader and validator
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a TrajectoryReader that loads a trajectory directory, validates every event against the Recorder's schema, and exposes iteration + filtering helpers, so downstream components operate on validated data without re-parsing files.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/trajectory_reader.py exposes TrajectoryReader class
Constructor takes a trajectory directory path; raises FileNotFoundError with a helpful message if missing
Loads metadata.json and validates it against contracts/trajectory.schema.json (uses the recorder's exposed schema module, imported as a package dep not copy-pasted)
Loads events.jsonl line-by-line; each event validated against trajectory schema; validation errors include line number and event seq
iter_events() -> Iterator[Event] yields validated events in seq order
iter_events_by_type(type: str) -> Iterator[Event] filters by event type
get_screenshot_path(seq: int) -> Path | None returns path to screenshots/NNNN.png if it exists
summary() -> dict returns {event_count, duration_ms, app_focus_history, click_count, text_input_count, app_switch_count, keyframe_count}
Handles trajectories with missing screenshots gracefully (logs a warning, returns None from get_screenshot_path)
Read-only semantics — never mutates trajectory files; opening files uses mode 'r' explicitly
tests/synthesizer/test_trajectory_reader.py: reads each of the 5 fixture trajectories, asserts summary() returns plausible numbers
tests/synthesizer/test_trajectory_reader.py: malformed events.jsonl raises with line number and event seq in the error message
tests/synthesizer/test_trajectory_reader.py: missing screenshot returns None, does not crash
tests/synthesizer/test_trajectory_reader.py: metadata.json with extra keys is accepted (forward-compat); missing required keys raises
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Depends on R-002 having merged to main. If contracts/trajectory.schema.json has changed since the recorder shipped, update BOTH this reader and the recorder in lockstep — do not fork the schema. Importing the recorder's schema module via the uv workspace is preferred over copying it.

userStories.title: Trajectory preprocessor: collapsing, downsampling, and keyframe selection
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need to preprocess a trajectory before sending it to Claude — collapsing long scroll/idle runs, selecting the 20 most informative keyframes, and producing a structured digest — so the LLM call fits in context and focuses on meaningful events.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/preprocess.py exposes preprocess_trajectory(reader: TrajectoryReader) -> PreprocessedTrajectory
Collapses consecutive scroll events in the same app within 3 seconds into a single synthetic 'scroll_run' event with payload {total_delta, duration_ms}
Collapses runs of 5+ seconds of no events into a synthetic 'idle' event with payload {duration_ms}
Selects up to 20 keyframes using priority: (1) one per app_switch, (2) one before each distinct click cluster, (3) filler keyframes at equal time intervals if under 20
Produces an ordered list of DigestEntry objects, each with {seq, timestamp_ms, summary_text (1 short natural-language sentence describing the event), screenshot_ref (None or path), app_bundle_id}
Summary text generation is deterministic (no LLM call here) — e.g., click with target.label 'Send' → 'Clicked button labeled \"Send\" in Gmail'
Preserves every click event unmodified (clicks are never collapsed — they are the atomic unit of a workflow)
Preserves every text_input event unmodified
Preserves every app_switch unmodified
Returns statistics: {original_event_count, digest_entry_count, screenshots_included, estimated_input_tokens}
tests/synthesizer/test_preprocess.py: scroll collapsing (generate 50 synthetic scroll events in 2 seconds, assert single scroll_run in digest)
tests/synthesizer/test_preprocess.py: idle detection (inject 10-second gap, assert idle event)
tests/synthesizer/test_preprocess.py: keyframe selection at 20 cap (inject 100 keyframes, assert exactly 20 selected with priority order respected)
tests/synthesizer/test_preprocess.py: clicks and text_inputs never dropped (fixture with 30 clicks + 10 text_inputs → all present in digest)
tests/synthesizer/test_preprocess.py: estimated_input_tokens within 20% of a tiktoken-style count (use a rough heuristic; do not take on the tiktoken dep — 4 chars per token approximation is fine)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is where 'does synthesis fit in context' is actually decided. Err toward including more clicks over more screenshots — clicks are the semantic backbone, screenshots are context. A 10-minute recording should digest down to < 100k input tokens before screenshots, comfortably within Sonnet's context.

userStories.title: Anthropic client wrapper with cost logging, retry, and fake mode
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a thin Anthropic client wrapper that handles API key loading, cost logging to costs.jsonl, exponential backoff on 429/500-class errors, and a 'fake mode' for tests that doesn't hit the real API, so LLM calls are observable and testable.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/llm_client.py exposes LLMClient class
Constructor reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment; raises ConfigurationError with a helpful message if missing
Supports TRACE_LLM_FAKE_MODE=1 env var — when set, uses a deterministic fake client that returns a canned response loaded from fixtures/llm_responses/*.json based on an input hash
TRACE_LLM_FAKE_MODE is AUTOMATICALLY enabled in test contexts (pytest fixture forces it on)
complete(messages, system, tools=None, max_tokens=4096) -> LLMResponse where LLMResponse has: text, stop_reason, input_tokens, output_tokens, raw (the full response)
On 429/500/502/503/504, retries with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, then fail) — max 5 retries
On 401, fails fast with a clear 'check ANTHROPIC_API_KEY' error (no retry)

After every successful call, appends a line to ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/costs.jsonl: {timestamp_iso, module: 'synthesizer', model, input_tokens, output_tokens, cost_estimate_usd, context_label}
cost_estimate_usd computed from a simple table in the module (Sonnet 4.5 input/output $/MTok); table is a constant dict, easy to update
Fake mode also logs to costs.jsonl with cost_estimate_usd=0 and model='fake' so cost tests can distinguish real vs fake calls
tests/synthesizer/test_llm_client.py: fake mode returns the canned response for a known input hash
tests/synthesizer/test_llm_client.py: retry test — using respx, fail twice with 429, then succeed, assert call count 3 and final response
tests/synthesizer/test_llm_client.py: 401 test — respx returns 401, assert fails fast without retry
tests/synthesizer/test_llm_client.py: cost logging test — after a call, costs.jsonl has a line with the right module and token counts
tests/synthesizer/test_llm_client.py: env var ANTHROPIC_API_KEY missing → ConfigurationError on construction
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Fake mode is non-negotiable — without it, every test costs money and is non-deterministic. The Ralph loop for this module will invoke LLMClient hundreds of times during iteration; the API-key env check + fake mode default ensures the Ralph loop never bills real API calls unless explicitly enabled.

userStories.title: Draft generation prompt and multimodal call
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a draft-generation system prompt and a multimodal Claude call that consumes a PreprocessedTrajectory (digest + screenshots) and produces a draft SKILL.md + skill.meta.json + up to 5 follow-up questions, so phase 1 of synthesis works.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/draft_prompt.py exposes DRAFT_SYSTEM_PROMPT constant (the system prompt Claude uses for draft generation)

DRAFT_SYSTEM_PROMPT instructs: only produce steps that correspond to events in the digest; flag destructive steps with the exact prefix '⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE]'; extract parameters using {name} syntax where values genuinely vary; produce up to 5 follow-up questions and no more; return output as a single JSON object with keys {markdown, meta, questions}
Few-shot examples: 2 complete example digest→draft pairs are included in the system prompt (checked in as fixtures/prompt_examples/*.txt and loaded at module import)
services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/draft.py exposes generate_draft(preprocessed: PreprocessedTrajectory, client: LLMClient) -> DraftResult
generate_draft builds a user message with: (a) a structured text section listing every digest entry in order, (b) image blocks for each included keyframe screenshot (base64-encoded, max 20)
Calls client.complete with the draft system prompt and a max_tokens of 4096
Parses the returned JSON; on parse failure, retries once with a 'your previous response was not valid JSON; please return a single JSON object with keys markdown, meta, questions' followup
Validates the returned markdown is parseable by skill_doc.parse_skill_md; on failure, retries once with the parse error fed back
Validates the returned meta passes schema.validate_meta and schema.validate_meta_against_markdown; on failure, retries once with the validation error fed back
Hard cap: max 3 total LLM calls per generate_draft invocation (initial + 2 retries); fails with DraftGenerationError afterwards
Returns DraftResult with {markdown, meta, questions, llm_calls: int, total_cost_usd: float}
tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py (with fake-mode LLMClient): canned response for each of the 5 reference trajectories produces a valid DraftResult
tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py: malformed LLM response (not JSON) → retry → success on second call
tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py: malformed markdown in response → retry → success on second call
tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py: 3 consecutive malformed responses → DraftGenerationError raised

tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py: parameter extraction test — a digest with text_input 'jake@example.com' in one field produces a draft where the email is either a parameter OR a follow-up question asks whether to parameterize it (either is acceptable)

tests/synthesizer/test_draft.py: destructive flagging test — digest with click on 'Send' button produces a draft with that step marked ⚠️ [DESTRUCTIVE] AND meta.destructive_steps includes that step number
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the LLM-heaviest story. The prompt is load-bearing — iterate on it against the fake-mode canned responses, then do ONE real-mode run per reference trajectory to validate the prompt works on the real API (this real-mode run is part of S-017, not this story). The two retry hooks (JSON parse, markdown parse) are cheap insurance against model drift and should not be removed.

userStories.title: Destructive keyword secondary matcher (belt-and-suspenders)
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, even if the LLM forgets to flag a destructive step, I need a secondary keyword matcher that independently scans every step's associated click target label (from the source trajectory) and flags steps whose labels contain destructive keywords, so the final skill file never has an unflagged destructive step due to LLM mistakes.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/destructive_matcher.py exposes DESTRUCTIVE_KEYWORDS constant: a list of strings (send, submit, delete, remove, publish, post, purchase, pay, buy, transfer, confirm, authorize, approve, share) — sourced from CLAUDE.md
exposes apply_destructive_matcher(parsed: ParsedSkill, reader: TrajectoryReader) -> ParsedSkill that returns a new ParsedSkill with destructive flags set where keywords match
Matching is case-insensitive word-boundary matches against click event target.label (NOT against step text, to avoid false positives from natural language like 'submit a query')
A step can only be destructive-flagged by this matcher if it has a resolvable source click event in the trajectory (mapping step N -> click event by sequential click order)
Matcher is ADDITIVE only — it never removes a destructive flag the LLM set. If there's disagreement, the matcher's flag wins and the step is flagged destructive.
Returns a MatcherReport with {added_flags: list[step_number], unchanged: list[step_number], llm_flags: list[step_number]} for telemetry
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: each of the 14 keywords is matched as a word boundary (not substring — 'sender' does not match 'send')
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: LLM missed a 'Send' click — matcher adds the flag, MatcherReport.added_flags contains the step number
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: LLM correctly flagged 'Delete' — matcher leaves it alone, MatcherReport.unchanged contains the step
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: step text contains 'Submit a ticket' but click target label is 'Ticket form' — NOT flagged (label, not step text, drives matching)
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: Non-click steps (keyboard shortcut, scroll) are never flagged by this matcher (they don't have target labels)
tests/synthesizer/test_destructive_matcher.py: i18n sanity — 'Envoyer' is NOT matched because we only match English keywords in v1 (documented behavior, not a bug)
Integration with S-007: after generate_draft, apply_destructive_matcher runs, and the final DraftResult reflects the combined destructive set
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the 'Runner safety' insurance policy at the synthesizer layer. The Runner has its OWN live-execution keyword matcher as a third line of defense, but adding it here means the skill file on disk is already correct. The i18n limitation is a documented non-goal for v1.

userStories.title: Slug generation and collision handling
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need to generate a URL-safe, filesystem-safe slug from a skill name, handle collisions with existing skills, and let the user pick a custom slug via the Q&A, so skills have predictable paths.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/slug.py exposes slugify(name: str) -> str producing a slug matching ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]{2,39}$
slugify lowercases, replaces spaces/hyphens with underscores, strips non-ASCII (or transliterates best-effort), removes all non [a-z0-9_] characters, truncates to 40 chars
slugify raises SlugError with a clear message if the input produces an empty or too-short (< 3 chars) result
resolve_unique_slug(base: str, skills_dir: Path) -> str returns the base if available, else base_2, base_3, etc. (not _v2 or _copy — numeric suffixes only)
validate_user_slug(user_input: str, skills_dir: Path) -> tuple[bool, str] returns (True, slug) if valid and available; (False, reason) with a human-readable message if not
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: slugify test cases including punctuation, accents, numbers-at-start, emoji (10+ cases)
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: empty result raises SlugError
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: resolve_unique_slug with 5 existing collisions produces base_6
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: validate_user_slug rejects invalid patterns (starts with digit, contains hyphen, too short, too long, collides with existing)
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: validate_user_slug accepts valid patterns
tests/synthesizer/test_slug.py: slugify output is always safe to use as a directory name on macOS (no reserved chars, no trailing dots)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The numeric-suffix-only rule is deliberate — avoids the _v2 / _copy / _new ambiguity that plagues file systems. The user can always specify a custom slug during Q&A to avoid the suffix.

userStories.title: Skill writer with atomic disk write and preview screenshots
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a SkillWriter that, given a final ParsedSkill + meta + source TrajectoryReader, writes SKILL.md and skill.meta.json atomically to disk, copies 3–5 preview screenshots from the trajectory, and registers the skill in the SQLite index, so the Runner and UI have a consistent view of available skills.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/writer.py exposes SkillWriter class
write(parsed: ParsedSkill, meta: dict, reader: TrajectoryReader, skills_root: Path) -> WrittenSkill
Runs final validation before writing: parse_skill_md(render_skill_md(parsed)) round-trip passes; validate_meta passes; validate_meta_against_markdown passes; slug is valid and unique

Atomic write sequence: write SKILL.md.tmp → fsync → write skill.meta.json.tmp → fsync → rename both → copy screenshots → commit to SQLite index. On any failure before commit, removes .tmp files and does NOT register in index.

Preview screenshot selection: up to 5, spread across the trajectory timeline — first keyframe, one near the 25% mark, one near 50%, one near 75%, and the final keyframe. Copies (not symlinks) from trajectory's screenshots/ to skill's preview/
Preview screenshots are re-numbered 01.png..05.png regardless of their trajectory seq
Directory perms 0700 on the new skill directory
Registers in index.db: (slug, name, trajectory_id, created_at, step_count, destructive_step_count)
Returns WrittenSkill with {slug, path, preview_paths: list, cost_total_usd: float (summed from the session)}
write() is idempotent per slug — calling again with an existing slug raises SkillAlreadyExistsError (caller must resolve slug first)
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: round-trip write then read the resulting SKILL.md — parsed output matches original ParsedSkill
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: write with a colliding slug raises SkillAlreadyExistsError
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: partial write crash simulation (mock fsync to raise on meta.json.tmp) — no skill directory left behind, no index entry
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: preview screenshots are copies, not symlinks (verify with stat)
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: dir perms 0700 on the skill directory and any subdirectories
tests/synthesizer/test_writer.py: trajectory with only 2 keyframes produces a preview/ with 2 screenshots (not 5 padded with duplicates)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Atomic-write-then-register is the correct ordering. If we registered first and the file write failed, the index would lie.

userStories.title: Synthesis session state machine
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, I need a SynthesisSession object that tracks the state of an in-progress synthesis (draft generated, question N of M answered, final draft approved), so the HTTP API can be resumed across multiple requests and the UI can show progress.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/session.py exposes SynthesisSession class
States: 'generating_draft', 'awaiting_answer', 'generating_revision', 'awaiting_approval', 'saving', 'completed', 'cancelled', 'errored'
State transitions are explicit — illegal transitions raise IllegalStateTransition
Constructor takes trajectory_id, loads TrajectoryReader, kicks off draft generation asynchronously
Stores: session_id (uuid), state, trajectory_id, draft (ParsedSkill + meta), questions (list), answered (list of (question_id, answer_text)), error (str | None), costs_so_far_usd
answer_question(question_id, text) transitions awaiting_answer → generating_revision → awaiting_answer (or → awaiting_approval if no more questions)
approve() transitions awaiting_approval → saving → completed; writes the skill via SkillWriter; returns WrittenSkill
cancel() transitions from any non-terminal state → cancelled; no skill written
Sessions are stored in an in-memory dict keyed by session_id; a background cleanup task evicts sessions older than 30 minutes in state != completed/cancelled (with a log warning per evicted session)
Cleanup task is cancellable for tests

Session persists the partial draft to ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/synthesis_sessions/<session_id>/draft.json after each revision (survives process restart for the current session; not required to resume after restart in v1 — documented limitation)
tests/synthesizer/test_session.py: full happy path — create session, answer 3 questions, approve, get WrittenSkill; all state transitions logged
tests/synthesizer/test_session.py: illegal transition — answer_question called while state is 'generating_draft' → IllegalStateTransition
tests/synthesizer/test_session.py: cancel at each non-terminal state → state becomes 'cancelled', no skill on disk
tests/synthesizer/test_session.py: error path — revision LLM call fails with DraftGenerationError → state becomes 'errored' with error set
tests/synthesizer/test_session.py: cleanup evicts a session older than 30 minutes (with fake time)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: In-memory session state is acceptable for v1 (single-user local app). Do NOT introduce a session database — it's overkill. The draft.json persistence on disk is a defense against losing user work on a synthesizer service crash, not a full resume mechanism.

userStories.title: Revision generation with answered questions
userStories.description: As the synthesizer, when the user answers a follow-up question, I need a revision Claude call that takes the current draft + the question + the answer and produces a revised draft, so the Q&A phase actually refines the output.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/revise.py exposes generate_revision(current_draft: DraftResult, question: Question, answer: str, client: LLMClient) -> DraftResult

System prompt for revision (services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/revise_prompt.py) is separate from the draft prompt; instructs Claude to update the markdown, meta, and remaining questions based on the answer, and to return the SAME JSON shape as the draft call
User message includes: the current markdown, the current meta JSON, the current question + the user's answer, and the remaining unanswered questions
Returns a DraftResult with an updated markdown, meta, and a possibly-pruned question list (Claude may remove questions rendered moot by the answer)
Same validation + retry policy as S-007: JSON parse retry, markdown parse retry, max 3 LLM calls per revision call

Parameter consistency rule: if the user's answer introduces a new parameter (e.g., 'that email address should always be X@example.com' → convert to literal), the revision must update both markdown AND meta consistently (enforced via validate_meta_against_markdown post-revision)
Cost is added to DraftResult.total_cost_usd cumulatively across draft + all revisions
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py (fake-mode): each of the 5 question categories (parameterization, intent, destructive, precondition, naming) produces a sensible revision from a canned response

tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: answer that removes a parameter (user says 'always send to jake@example.com') → revised markdown has literal email, revised meta.parameters does not include recipient_email
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: answer that confirms a destructive step ('yes, always ask before sending') → step remains destructive, no change to flags
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: answer that renames the skill → revised meta.name updated, slug NOT regenerated here (slug is finalized at write time)
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: LLM returns invalid meta → retry with error fed back → success
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: destructive matcher re-applied after revision (LLM may have accidentally unflagged something; matcher restores it)
tests/synthesizer/test_revise.py: empty answer string → raises ValidationError before any LLM call
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Re-running the destructive matcher after every revision is critical — the LLM might inadvertently remove a ⚠️ marker while restructuring text. This is paranoia but cheap paranoia.

userStories.title: FastAPI routes for synthesis and skills management
userStories.description: As the Tauri frontend, I need HTTP endpoints to start a synthesis session, stream updates via SSE, submit answers, approve and save, list skills, and delete skills, so the UI can drive the synthesizer and display the skill library.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/api.py exposes a FastAPI APIRouter mounted at / (the gateway prefixes it with /synthesize, /skills as appropriate)
POST /synthesize/start body {trajectory_id: string} → {session_id} (state starts as generating_draft; draft generation happens in the background)

GET /synthesize/{session_id}/stream → SSE stream; events: 'state_change', 'draft_ready', 'question_ready', 'revision_ready', 'saved', 'error'; keeps connection open until session reaches a terminal state
GET /synthesize/{session_id} → current session snapshot {state, draft, questions, answered, next_question, costs_so_far_usd}
POST /synthesize/{session_id}/answer body {question_id, answer} → 200 on accepted (revision runs async; UI should reconnect to /stream for result)
POST /synthesize/{session_id}/approve body {slug?: string} → {skill_slug, path} on success; 409 if slug collides and user didn't provide override
DELETE /synthesize/{session_id} → {cancelled: true}
GET /skills → list of {slug, name, created_at, step_count, destructive_step_count} from index.db
GET /skills/{slug} → full skill: {markdown, meta, preview_urls}
GET /skills/{slug}/preview/{filename} → serves preview PNG
DELETE /skills/{slug} → 200 if exists, 404 otherwise; removes directory AND index row atomically (index first, then directory — so a partial delete doesn't leave a ghost row)
All endpoints bind to 127.0.0.1 ONLY (inherits gateway config from CLAUDE.md rule)

tests/synthesizer/test_api.py: full synthesis happy path via httpx.AsyncClient with fake-mode LLM (start → stream draft_ready → answer question → stream revision_ready → approve → GET /skills shows new skill)
tests/synthesizer/test_api.py: stream sends a keepalive comment every 15 seconds (SSE :ping convention) so proxies don't close the connection
tests/synthesizer/test_api.py: unknown session_id returns 404 from every endpoint
tests/synthesizer/test_api.py: DELETE /skills/{slug} followed by GET /skills does not show the skill
tests/synthesizer/test_api.py: trajectory_id that doesn't exist → POST /synthesize/start returns 404
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: SSE keepalives matter — without them, some proxies kill the connection at 30-60s idle. This is for the Tauri app's local HTTP, not a real proxy, but it's good practice and cheap.

userStories.title: Snapshot similarity test harness for semantic comparison
userStories.description: As the module's acceptance signal, I need a snapshot similarity test that compares a synthesized SKILL.md against a 'golden' hand-crafted version, using a small secondary LLM call to score semantic equivalence (not exact match), so the module's Ralph loop has a meaningful pass/fail signal despite LLM non-determinism.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/similarity.py exposes score_skill_similarity(generated: ParsedSkill, golden: ParsedSkill, client: LLMClient) -> SimilarityScore
SimilarityScore has: overall (float 0.0-1.0), step_coverage (float), parameter_match (float), destructive_match (float), reasoning (str)
Uses a single Claude Haiku 4.5 call (cheaper than Sonnet for rubric scoring) with a rubric prompt

Rubric prompt asks the model to score: are both skills describing the same workflow? (coverage); do they have the same parameterization? (parameter_match); do they have the same destructive steps? (destructive_match)
Returns a structured JSON response; parses strictly

If the two skills have structural disagreements (different number of parameters, different destructive_steps count), those dimensions are scored structurally (0.0 or 1.0 based on exact match) rather than asking the LLM — faster and more reliable for binary facts
tests/synthesizer/test_similarity.py (fake-mode): identical skills score ≥ 0.95 on overall
tests/synthesizer/test_similarity.py (fake-mode): skills differing only in wording score ≥ 0.85
tests/synthesizer/test_similarity.py (fake-mode): skills differing in destructive step count score destructive_match < 1.0
tests/synthesizer/test_similarity.py (fake-mode): skills missing a step score step_coverage < 0.8
A pytest helper @pytest.fixture similarity_scorer is exposed in conftest.py for use by S-017
Cost guard: each similarity call logs cost; test asserts cost per call < $0.02 (Haiku is cheap)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Using Haiku for this evaluator is a deliberate cost choice. The rubric is simple enough that Haiku handles it well; Sonnet would be 5x the cost for no material accuracy gain. The structural dimensions (parameter count, destructive count) don't need an LLM at all — compute directly.

userStories.title: Integration test: full synthesis pipeline end-to-end (fake-mode)
userStories.description: As a confidence check, I need an integration test that drives the entire synthesis pipeline — from a trajectory directory on disk, through draft → Q&A → revision → approval → written skill — using fake-mode LLM responses, so the whole pipeline is exercised without real API calls.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/synthesizer/test_e2e_fake.py runs the full pipeline for each of the 5 fixture trajectories

For each fixture: loads trajectory, runs preprocess, calls generate_draft (fake-mode), applies destructive matcher, simulates 2 Q&A rounds with canned answers, approves, writes skill, verifies skill directory on disk
Asserts written SKILL.md parses back to a ParsedSkill equivalent to the final draft
Asserts skill.meta.json validates against the schema
Asserts preview/ has ≤5 PNGs that are valid images (Pillow can open)
Asserts cost logging: costs.jsonl gained at least 3 entries (draft + 2 revisions) for this session, all with model='fake'
Asserts no real API calls were made (respx should catch any attempt to hit api.anthropic.com)
Asserts the SQLite index shows the new skill
Asserts cleanup: cancelling an in-progress session removes the synthesis_sessions/ directory for that session
Test runs in under 10 seconds per fixture (no artificial delays)
tests/synthesizer/test_e2e_fake.py: test_error_path — synthesizer configured with a malformed canned response for 3 retries → session ends in 'errored' state, NO skill written, NO index entry
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: Fake-mode is what makes this test runnable in Ralph's sandbox without an API key. Real-mode verification happens in S-017.

userStories.title: Golden skill fixtures for the 5 reference workflows
userStories.description: As the similarity test's ground truth, I need hand-crafted 'golden' SKILL.md + skill.meta.json files for each of the 5 reference workflows, committed to the repo, so the snapshot test has something to compare synthesized output against.
acceptanceCriteria: fixtures/skills/gmail_reply/SKILL.md exists with full strict-format content
fixtures/skills/gmail_reply/skill.meta.json exists and passes schema validation
Same for calendar_block, finder_organize, slack_status, notes_daily
Each golden skill includes at least 4 steps
Each golden skill has at least one parameter and at least one destructive step (where applicable — calendar_block and notes_daily may have 0 destructive, that's fine)
fixtures/skills/README.md documents the purpose of these files and the rule: DO NOT let a Ralph iteration regenerate these automatically — they are hand-crafted ground truth
The round-trip property holds: parse_skill_md(render_skill_md(parse_skill_md(f))) == parse_skill_md(f) for each golden file
validate_meta_against_markdown passes for each pair
A script scripts/check_fixtures.sh validates all fixtures pass schema + round-trip + cross-check; runs in < 5 seconds; exits non-zero on any failure
scripts/check_fixtures.sh is wired into the quality gates (Ralph iterations run it before committing)
tests/synthesizer/test_golden_fixtures.py iterates and validates all 5
Total size of fixtures/skills/ is under 1MB (no screenshots in fixtures — those live in fixtures/trajectories/)
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: These are hand-crafted. Ralph is NOT allowed to regenerate them from trajectories — if it does, the snapshot test becomes tautological. Document this rule in the fixtures README AND call it out in CLAUDE.md.

userStories.title: Real-API smoke test (opt-in, not part of the Ralph loop)
userStories.description: As a pre-merge verification step, I need a real-API smoke test that runs draft generation against real Claude Sonnet 4.5 for each of the 5 reference trajectories, compares against the golden skills via similarity scoring, and asserts semantic equivalence — gated behind an env var so it does not run during Ralph iteration.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/synthesizer/test_real_smoke.py exists and runs only when TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS=1 is set in the environment
The test is decorated @pytest.mark.skipif(not os.environ.get('TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS'), reason='Set TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS=1 to run')
For each of the 5 fixture trajectories, runs the full draft generation (NOT Q&A — Q&A involves human answers) against the real API
Scores each draft against the golden skill using score_skill_similarity (using Haiku)
Asserts overall ≥ 0.80 on at least 4 of 5 fixtures (LLM drift tolerance)
Asserts destructive_match == 1.0 on all 5 fixtures (destructive flagging must be exact)
Reports total cost across all 5 fixture generations; asserts under $2 total (caps runaway cost)
Writes a report to tests/synthesizer/smoke_report.json with per-fixture scores and timestamp
tests/synthesizer/README.md documents how to run this test (TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS=1 uv run pytest tests/synthesizer/test_real_smoke.py) and what the expected costs are
CLAUDE.md's Synthesizer section references this smoke test as the pre-merge gate
This test DOES NOT block the Ralph completion promise (Ralph emits SYNTHESIZER_DONE based on fake-mode tests only)
Human runs this test before merging feat/synthesizer to main; a passing smoke_report.json is attached to the PR description
userStories.notes: This is the Synthesizer's analog to the Recorder's smoke test — except here it's a small, cheap real-API test rather than a hardware test. The 4-of-5 threshold on overall similarity tolerates LLM variance; the 5-of-5 on destructive_match does not, because destructive flagging is safety-critical and must be deterministic (the secondary keyword matcher enforces this).

userStories.title: Cost budget guardrails and alerting
userStories.description: As a safety measure, I need cost tracking that alerts the user if synthesis costs exceed configurable budgets, so a runaway prompt or a stuck session doesn't quietly rack up bills.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/src/synthesizer/budget.py exposes BudgetMonitor class
Reads budget config from ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/config.json: {synthesis_per_session_usd_cap: float (default 1.00), synthesis_daily_usd_cap: float (default 5.00)}
check_session_cost(session_id, current_cost) -> BudgetStatus (OK, WARNING at 80%, EXCEEDED at 100%)
When session cost exceeds per-session cap, synthesis session transitions to 'errored' with error 'per-session cost cap exceeded'
check_daily_cost reads costs.jsonl, sums today's synthesizer entries; when over daily cap, POST /synthesize/start returns 429 with a clear message
Logs a warning to stderr at 80% of either cap
tests/synthesizer/test_budget.py: per-session cap exceeded → session errors
tests/synthesizer/test_budget.py: daily cap exceeded → start returns 429
tests/synthesizer/test_budget.py: config.json missing → defaults used
tests/synthesizer/test_budget.py: config.json with invalid values → ConfigurationError on load
tests/synthesizer/test_budget.py: cost accumulation across multiple sessions in a single day
Integration with S-011: BudgetMonitor is checked before each LLM call in a synthesis session
Integration with S-013: daily cap check is middleware-level on POST /synthesize/start
mypy --strict passes
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: The per-session cap is the first line of defense against a retry loop going wild. The daily cap is the second line. Ralph iterations should have a LOWER cap (e.g., $1/day) set via the dev profile config; the Ralph loop prompt should ensure it sets this before iteration.

userStories.title: End-to-end schema cross-check and lint across all modules
userStories.description: As a consistency guarantee, I need a single script that validates all shipped contracts (trajectory schema, skill-meta schema) are internally consistent and that fixture files across modules (trajectories in fixtures/trajectories, skills in fixtures/skills) are mutually compatible, so breaking changes are caught at CI time.
acceptanceCriteria: scripts/check_contracts.sh exists and validates: (1) all JSON schemas in contracts/ are valid JSON Schema draft 2020-12, (2) every trajectory in fixtures/trajectories/ passes trajectory schema, (3) every skill in fixtures/skills/ passes skill-meta schema AND the round-trip/cross-check invariants, (4) every golden skill's trajectory_id refers to an existing fixture trajectory
Exits non-zero on any failure with a specific message pointing to the offending file and issue
Runs in under 10 seconds on the full fixture set
Is wired into the Ralph loop's quality gates (CLAUDE.md update: add this to the list of commands every iteration runs before committing)
tests/synthesizer/test_contract_check.py invokes the script via subprocess and asserts exit code 0
tests/synthesizer/test_contract_check.py: injects a malformed fixture temporarily → script exits non-zero → restore fixture → exits 0
scripts/check_contracts.sh works from the repo root regardless of which branch is checked out
A failing check produces output an LLM (or human) can act on — includes file path, line number if possible, and the specific invariant that failed
mypy --strict passes on any Python in the script
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the cross-module consistency check. Without it, a Ralph iteration on the Synthesizer could unknowingly produce a fixture skill referencing a trajectory_id that was deleted from fixtures/trajectories.

userStories.title: CLAUDE.md update and AGENTS.md for synthesizer module
userStories.description: As a long-running project convention, after this branch merges I need the repo-root CLAUDE.md updated with any learnings and a services/synthesizer/AGENTS.md file created with module-specific context, so future Ralph iterations (including on other branches) benefit from discovered patterns.
acceptanceCriteria: services/synthesizer/AGENTS.md exists and contains sections: 'Purpose', 'Key files', 'Prompt engineering notes', 'Common gotchas', 'How to run tests'

'Prompt engineering notes' lists any specific lessons learned during Ralph iteration — e.g., 'Claude sometimes forgets to include meta.destructive_steps; the matcher in S-008 handles this but consider prompt tweaks if it becomes frequent'
'Common gotchas' lists at least 3 concrete issues discovered during iteration (with specific fixes)
'How to run tests' documents: unit tests, fake-mode e2e, real-mode smoke, the TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS env var, and expected costs
CLAUDE.md at repo root updated in the 'Module architecture' section if any contract details drifted from initial write
progress.txt has append-only entries for each significant learning (one entry per Ralph iteration where something non-trivial was learned)
AGENTS.md references CLAUDE.md explicitly: 'See /CLAUDE.md for project-wide context, safety invariants, and reference workflows.'
Total AGENTS.md length is 100-400 lines — long enough to be useful, short enough not to bloat future Ralph contexts
No secrets, API keys, or user data in AGENTS.md
mypy --strict passes (no Python changes expected, so should be trivially green)
ruff check passes
pytest passes
userStories.notes: This is the last story on the branch and is the hand-off to future maintenance. Ralph iterations MUST update progress.txt incrementally as they go, not save it all for this story. Per the Ralph docs, AGENTS.md files are auto-read by Claude Code.

scope: Single-user local app — no multi-user sessions, no cloud persistence
Claude Sonnet 4.5 for drafts/revisions, Claude Haiku 4.5 for similarity scoring — no OpenAI or other providers
English only (parameter names and destructive keywords are English)
Up to 5 follow-up questions per session — hard cap
Up to 20 screenshots included per LLM call — enforced at preprocess
No skill editing UI in v1 — user can edit the .md file directly after synthesis

resources.apiCostBudgetForRalph: $5 total across all Ralph iterations — use fake-mode by default

aiLlm: Fake-mode is the default in any test context; TRACE_LLM_FAKE_MODE=1 is auto-set by pytest fixtures
Real API calls only happen when TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS=1 is explicitly set
Retry policy: exponential backoff on 429/5xx, max 5 retries; 401 fails fast
All calls logged to costs.jsonl with input/output tokens and cost estimate
Per-session and daily cost caps enforced (S-018)

security.network: FastAPI binds to 127.0.0.1 ONLY. Anthropic API calls go to api.anthropic.com over HTTPS with the official SDK. No other network destinations.
security.filesystem: Reads trajectories under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/trajectories/ (read-only). Writes skills under ~/Library/Application Support/Trace[-dev]/skills/ with dir perms 0700. No writes outside this tree.
security.secrets: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY read from environment only. Never logged. Never written to disk. Never returned in any API response.
security.dataHandling: Trajectory contents (including user-typed text) are sent to Anthropic during draft generation. This is documented in README as a v1 trade-off — the whole product assumes the user trusts Claude. NEVER sent anywhere else.
security.zeroDataRetention: Synthesizer does not set any special ZDR headers in v1 — rely on Anthropic account-level ZDR if the user has it. Documented.
ralphGuardrails: Ralph iterations use the dev profile: ~/Library/Application Support/Trace-dev/
Ralph iterations use a SEPARATE ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (the dev key) with a $20/month cap set in Anthropic console
Daily cost cap (S-018) is set to $1 in the dev config vs $5 in prod
Ralph iterations MUST NOT run tests/synthesizer/test_real_smoke.py — it's gated behind TRACE_REAL_API_TESTS and the Ralph prompt instructs not to set this

nonGoals: Skill editing in the UI (user edits .md file directly)
Skill composition (calling one skill from another)
Non-English languages (destructive keyword matcher is English-only)
Tracing/telemetry across synthesizer sessions (costs.jsonl is the only record)
Persisting in-progress sessions across process restarts (documented limitation — user must re-synthesize if service restarts mid-session)
Automatic regeneration of golden fixtures — these are hand-crafted on purpose

successMetrics.contractStability: No breaking changes to contracts/skill-meta.schema.json after S-002 is merged
successMetrics.fakeModeCoverage: All tests except test_real_smoke.py pass in fake mode with no network access
successMetrics.roundTripStability: parse_skill_md(render_skill_md(p)) == p for all 5 golden skills and all synthesized outputs in test_e2e_fake
successMetrics.smokeTestThreshold: overall similarity ≥ 0.80 on 4+ of 5 reference workflows in real-mode smoke
successMetrics.destructiveFlaggingAccuracy: destructive_match == 1.0 on all 5 reference workflows — non-negotiable (the secondary matcher enforces this)
successMetrics.averageSessionCost: < $0.15 per full synthesis on reference workflows (draft + 2 revisions)
successMetrics.cleanQualityGates: ruff, mypy --strict, pytest, and check_contracts.sh all pass at every commit

openQuestions: Is 5 questions the right hard cap? Might be too few for complex workflows; revisit after smoke test results.
Do we need a 'session resume' feature for the v1 release, or is 'if the synthesizer service crashes, you lose the in-progress session' acceptable? Current PRD says acceptable; flag for user feedback.
Should we support skill editing in the UI in v1.1 or leave it to the user's text editor? Blocked on observing actual user behavior.
Daily cost cap default of $5 — is that right for personal use? May need adjustment based on usage patterns.
Should golden fixtures be regenerated manually every N months to track prompt drift? Currently no process — flag for maintenance cycle.

If a trajectory has zero destructive click targets but the synthesizer's LLM flags one anyway, should the secondary matcher UN-flag it? Current design says no (LLM flags are trusted); revisit if false positives are observed in practice.
